segments.text:
B cell Development
Important Concepts:
B cell development occurs in the bone marrow, and is coupled with DNA recombination of the heavy and light chain loci
B-cells also have a B-cell receptor
Central self-tolerance is the prevention of self-reacting B-cells into the circulatin
All in the Bone marrow

Generation of functional Immunoglobulin
Involves recombination
Heavy
Light
Unproductive recombination events get a second chance

IgM membrane bound is expressed first
Poly A add sites
All in the Bone marrow

Immunoreceptor tyrosine-based activation motif (ITAM)
Ig-a/Ig-b
Immunoglobulin superfamily

B-Cell Receptor
Immunoreceptor tyrosine-based activation motif (ITAM)
Can you guess what is wrong with this illustration?

Self-reactive B cells are selected against in bone marrow
About 5 x 107 B-cells are produced per day but only about 10% of these make it into circulation.

Formation of “Central” Self-tolerance
Also have peripheral self-tolerance, and injury induced self-reactivity
Failure of self-tolerance results in Autoimmune disorders

Important ideas/concepts:
Antibody gene/bone marrow
B-cell receptor
B-cell self-tolerance/bone marrow
Autoimmune diseases
Secreted and membrane forms of Immunoglobulins

Note: can generate either IgM secreted, IgM membrane bound, or IgD secreted, IgD membrane bound by alternative splicing
Poly A add sites
Overview of B-cell Development and Activation

Upon completion of heavy chain rearrangement the cell is classified as a pre-B cell
Upon completion of light chain rearrangement the cell is classified as an immature B cell
(Mature, but still naïve)
Hint: I would know this slide
Both lymphocytes exit to periphery with re-arranged receptors and are “naïve”

Naïve
Denoting B and T cells that have not encountered antigen; synonymous with “unprimed” and “virgin”
B cell activation and proliferation
Naïve B cell				 	Plasma cell
Most antibody production by activated B-cells is “thymus dependent”
B-cell response to thymus-dependent antigens requires direct contact with TH cells, not simply exposure to TH derived cytokines.
Contact between B and T cells

“Whereas antigen-presenting cells such as dendritic cells and macrophages are omnivorous and ingest antigens nonspecifically, a B-cell generally presents only an antigen that it specifically recognizes.

“B-cells could be regarded as helping their helpers because the antigenic peptides produced within the endocytic processing pathway associate with class II MHC molecules and are presented on the B-cell membrane to the TH cell, inducing its activation.”

B-Cells		T-Cells
Signal 1
Receptor activation: 		Antigen binding to and 	Antigen and MHC binding
cross-linking membrane 	to T-Cell receptor
bound antibody (mIg)
Co-receptor activation: 	B-cell co-receptor CR2 (CD21)	CD4 or CD8 binding to MHC
binding to C3d
Signal 2		B-cell CD40		CD28 on Helper T cell binds to
binds to T-cell CD40L		B7 (CD80/86) on antigen 						presenting cell
Inhibitory Signal		CD22			CTLA-4
Signal 1 induced in part by antigen binding and cross linking mIg

B-Cell co-receptor
Note:
-C3d coating of antigen is recognized by B cell co-receptor
-the co-receptor binding is similar to CD4/CD8 binding to MHC
A 302-amino-acid fragment of C3b.

Thymus Dependent activation (TD)
CD21
(CR2)
“Once activated, the helper T cell can then activate a B cell that specifically displays the same complex of foreign peptide and class II MHC protein on its surface.”

Primary Organs
Bone Marrow
Thymus
Secondary Organs
Lymph nodes
Spleen
Mucosa Associated Lymphoid Tissues
Lymphatic system
Organs of the Immune System

mostly B cells, macrophages, and dendritic cells
mostly T cells and dendritic cells
many Plasma cells secreting antibodies (medullary cord)

Lymph nodes are efficient filters: ~90% antigen is removed
Note: T-cells are enriched in the paracortex
Note: B-cells are enriched in the cortex
Antigen specific T Cells and B cells interact at the boundary to form conjugates
Activated B and TH cells migrate to and from primary follicles
Affinity maturation, class switching, formation of plasma cells and memory B cells

Activation Induced cytidine Deaminase (AID)
Note: involved in Class Switching Recombination (CSR) and Somatic Hyper Mutation (SHM)
Somatic hypermutation (in Germinal Centers)

Secondary follicle
Germinal center
-environment for B cell maturation
mantle of small lymphocytes
Germinal centers are also found in the spleen, Peyer’s Patch, and  tonsils
Numerous cytokines generate signals for proliferation and class switching
Overall view of lymphatic system drainage

Spleen anatomy
Mostly T lymphocytes
Mostly B lymphocytes

MALT :
The term MALT really refers to all of the mucosal-associated lymph nodules and diffuse lymphatic cells that encompass the BALT (bronchi) and GALT (gastro), but also include lymphatic accumulations in other organ systems, such as the female reproductive tract. Essentially the lymphatic tissues of the lamina propria of the digestive system, the respiratory lymphatics and the reproductive tract would be included as MALT.
M=microfold cells
Humoral Response

Primary Response
Immune response following initial exposure to antigen; this response is characterized by short duration and low magnitude compared to response following subsequent exposures to the same antigen.

Secondary Response
Immune response following exposure to previously encountered antigen; the secondary response occurs more rapidly and is greater in magnitude and duration than the primary response
Immunization provides protection against disease
Concentration and Isotype of Serum Antibody Following Primary and Secondary Immunization with Antigen

The population of memory B cells specific for a given antigen is considerably larger than the population of corresponding naive B cells.  Furthermore, memory cells are more easily activated than naive B cells.
Some memory B-cells persist for the life of an individual

Important Terms, Concepts
Stromal cells
Pro-B cell, Pre-B cell, Immature B cell, naïve B cell, mature B cell
Thymus independent, thymus dependent activation
B-Cell Receptor (BCR)
Inhibitory signals
Primary Response
Secondary Response
Don’t forget the memory B-cells
B-cells enriched in cortex, T-cells in paracortex
Germinal center-affinity maturation, class switching, memory cell formation
